1→"""Tool call dependency graph construction.
     2→
     3→Infers causal relationships between tool calls within a session:
     4→read→edit chains, error→retry loops, search→read flows, and temporal
     5→sequencing.  Produces a DAG that reveals the agent's problem-solving
     6→strategy beyond a flat chronological list.
     7→"""
     8→
     9→from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
    10→
    11→from vibelens.analysis.constants import TOOL_CATEGORY_MAP
    12→from vibelens.models.trajectories import Step
    13→
    14→_FILE_PATH_KEYS = {"file_path", "path", "filename", "notebook_path", "directory"}
    15→
    16→
    17→class ToolEdge(BaseModel):
    18→    """Directed edge between two tool calls."""
    19→
    20→    source_tool_call_id: str = Field(description="ID of the preceding tool call.")
    21→    target_tool_call_id: str = Field(description="ID of the following tool call.")
    22→    relation: str = Field(
    23→        description="Edge type: read_before_write, error_retry, search_then_read, sequential."
    24→    )
    25→    shared_resource: str = Field(
    26→        default="", description="File path or pattern shared between source and target."
    27→    )
    28→
    29→
    30→class ToolDependencyGraph(BaseModel):
    31→    """DAG of causal dependencies between tool calls in a session."""
    32→
    33→    session_id: str = Field(description="Session this graph belongs to.")
    34→    nodes: list[str] = Field(
    35→        default_factory=list, description="Tool call IDs in topological order."
    36→    )
    37→    edges: list[ToolEdge] = Field(
    38→        default_factory=list, description="Directed edges between tool calls."
    39→    )
    40→    root_nodes: list[str] = Field(
    41→        default_factory=list, description="Tool call IDs with no predecessors."
    42→    )
    43→
    44→
    45→def build_tool_graph(steps: list[Step], session_id: str = "") -> ToolDependencyGraph:
    46→    """Build a dependency graph from a session's tool calls.
    47→
    48→    Args:
    49→        steps: Ordered steps from a single session.
    50→        session_id: Session identifier for the graph.
    51→
    52→    Returns:
    53→        ToolDependencyGraph with inferred edges.
    54→    """
    55→    flat_calls = _flatten_tool_calls(steps)
    56→    if not flat_calls:
    57→        return ToolDependencyGraph(session_id=session_id)
    58→    nodes = [tc_id for tc_id, _, _, _ in flat_calls]
    59→    edges: list[ToolEdge] = []
    60→    has_predecessor: set[str] = set()
    61→
    62→    edges.extend(_find_read_before_write(flat_calls, has_predecessor))
    63→    edges.extend(_find_error_retry(flat_calls, has_predecessor))
    64→    edges.extend(_find_search_then_read(flat_calls, has_predecessor))
    65→    edges.extend(_find_sequential(flat_calls, has_predecessor))
    66→
    67→    root_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n not in has_predecessor]
    68→
    69→    return ToolDependencyGraph(
    70→        session_id=session_id, nodes=nodes, edges=edges, root_nodes=root_nodes
    71→    )
    72→
    73→
    74→def _flatten_tool_calls(steps: list[Step]) -> list[tuple[str, str, str, dict | str | None]]:
    75→    """Extract (id, name, category, input) tuples in chronological order."""
    76→    result = []
    77→    for step in steps:
    78→        for tc in step.tool_calls:
    79→            if not tc.tool_call_id:
    80→                continue
    81→            category = TOOL_CATEGORY_MAP.get(tc.function_name, "other")
    82→            result.append((tc.tool_call_id, tc.function_name, category, tc.arguments))
    83→    return result
    84→
    85→
    86→def _extract_file_path(raw_input: dict | str | None) -> str:
    87→    """Extract a file path from tool input arguments."""
    88→    if not isinstance(raw_input, dict):
    89→        return ""
    90→    for key in _FILE_PATH_KEYS:
    91→        value = raw_input.get(key, "")
    92→        if isinstance(value, str) and value:
    93→            return value
    94→    return ""
    95→
    96→
    97→def _find_read_before_write(
    98→    calls: list[tuple[str, str, str, dict | str | None]], has_predecessor: set[str]
    99→) -> list[ToolEdge]:
   100→    """Detect Read(path=X) followed by Edit/Write(path=X)."""
   101→    edges: list[ToolEdge] = []
   102→    read_paths: dict[str, str] = {}
   103→    for tc_id, _name, category, raw_input in calls:
   104→        path = _extract_file_path(raw_input)
   105→        if not path:
   106→            continue
   107→        if category == "file_read":
   108→            read_paths[path] = tc_id
   109→        elif category == "file_write" and path in read_paths:
   110→            source_id = read_paths[path]
   111→            edges.append(
   112→                ToolEdge(
   113→                    source_tool_call_id=source_id,
   114→                    target_tool_call_id=tc_id,
   115→                    relation="read_before_write",
   116→                    shared_resource=path,
   117→                )
   118→            )
   119→            has_predecessor.add(tc_id)
   120→    return edges
   121→
   122→
   123→def _find_error_retry(
   124→    calls: list[tuple[str, str, str, dict | str | None]], has_predecessor: set[str]
   125→) -> list[ToolEdge]:
   126→    """Detect tool with is_error followed by same tool name.
   127→
   128→    NOTE: Currently inert — the flattened tuple doesn't carry ``is_error``,
   129→    so ``prev_error_name`` is never set. This is a placeholder for when
   130→    the tuple is extended to include error status. The sequential fallback
   131→    in ``_find_sequential`` covers the gap for now.
   132→    """
   133→    edges: list[ToolEdge] = []
   134→    prev_error_name: str = ""
   135→    prev_error_id: str = ""
   136→    for _idx, (tc_id, name, _category, _raw_input) in enumerate(calls):
   137→        if prev_error_name and name == prev_error_name:
   138→            edges.append(
   139→                ToolEdge(
   140→                    source_tool_call_id=prev_error_id,
   141→                    target_tool_call_id=tc_id,
   142→                    relation="error_retry",
   143→                )
   144→            )
   145→            has_predecessor.add(tc_id)
   146→            prev_error_name = ""
   147→            prev_error_id = ""
   148→            continue
   149→        prev_error_name = ""
   150→        prev_error_id = ""
   151→    return edges
   152→
   153→
   154→def _find_search_then_read(
   155→    calls: list[tuple[str, str, str, dict | str | None]], has_predecessor: set[str]
   156→) -> list[ToolEdge]:
   157→    """Detect Grep/Glob followed by Read targeting found paths."""
   158→    edges: list[ToolEdge] = []
   159→    last_search_id: str = ""
   160→    for tc_id, _name, category, raw_input in calls:
   161→        if category == "search":
   162→            last_search_id = tc_id
   163→        elif category == "file_read" and last_search_id:
   164→            edges.append(
   165→                ToolEdge(
   166→                    source_tool_call_id=last_search_id,
   167→                    target_tool_call_id=tc_id,
   168→                    relation="search_then_read",
   169→                    shared_resource=_extract_file_path(raw_input),
   170→                )
   171→            )
   172→            has_predecessor.add(tc_id)
   173→    return edges
   174→
   175→
   176→def _find_sequential(
   177→    calls: list[tuple[str, str, str, dict | str | None]], has_predecessor: set[str]
   178→) -> list[ToolEdge]:
   179→    """Add sequential edges for tool calls that weren't linked by stronger relations.
   180→
   181→    Only connects calls that don't already have a predecessor from
   182→    read_before_write, error_retry, or search_then_read — acting as
   183→    a fallback to ensure every non-root node has exactly one inbound edge.
   184→    """
   185→    edges: list[ToolEdge] = []
   186→    for i in range(1, len(calls)):
   187→        tc_id = calls[i][0]
   188→        if tc_id in has_predecessor:
   189→            continue
   190→        prev_id = calls[i - 1][0]
   191→        edges.append(
   192→            ToolEdge(source_tool_call_id=prev_id, target_tool_call_id=tc_id, relation="sequential")
   193→        )
   194→        has_predecessor.add(tc_id)
   195→    return edges
   196→

1→"""Session conversation phase detection.
     2→
     3→Classifies segments of a coding agent session into phases (exploration,
     4→implementation, debugging, verification, planning) using a sliding
     5→window over tool call categories.  Enables "what fraction of time is
     6→spent exploring vs coding?" analytics.
     7→"""
     8→
     9→from vibelens.analysis.constants import TOOL_CATEGORY_MAP
    10→from vibelens.ingest.parsers.base import is_error_content
    11→from vibelens.models.analysis.phase import PhaseSegment
    12→from vibelens.models.enums import SessionPhase
    13→from vibelens.models.trajectories import Step
    14→
    15→PHASE_WINDOW_SIZE = 5
    16→
    17→# 40% threshold balances sensitivity vs noise: lower values fragment
    18→# sessions into too many micro-phases, higher values miss genuine
    19→# phase transitions in mixed-activity windows.
    20→_DOMINANCE_THRESHOLD = 0.4
    21→
    22→
    23→def detect_phases(messages: list[Step]) -> list[PhaseSegment]:
    24→    """Detect conversation phases using a sliding window.
    25→
    26→    Args:
    27→        messages: Ordered messages from a single session.
    28→
    29→    Returns:
    30→        List of PhaseSegment objects covering the full session.
    31→    """
    32→    if not messages:
    33→        return []
    34→
    35→    window_phases = _classify_windows(messages)
    36→    if not window_phases:
    37→        return [_make_segment(messages, 0, len(messages) - 1, SessionPhase.PLANNING)]
    38→
    39→    raw_segments = _merge_adjacent(window_phases, messages)
    40→    return _absorb_small_segments(raw_segments)
    41→
    42→
    43→def _classify_windows(messages: list[Step]) -> list[tuple[int, SessionPhase]]:
    44→    """Classify each window position by its dominant tool category.
    45→
    46→    Returns:
    47→        List of (start_index, phase) tuples.
    48→    """
    49→    results: list[tuple[int, SessionPhase]] = []
    50→    for start in range(len(messages)):
    51→        end = min(start + PHASE_WINDOW_SIZE, len(messages))
    52→        window = messages[start:end]
    53→        phase = _classify_window(window)
    54→        results.append((start, phase))
    55→    return results
    56→
    57→
    58→def _classify_window(window: list[Step]) -> SessionPhase:
    59→    """Classify a single window of messages into a phase."""
    60→    category_counts: dict[str, int] = {}
    61→    total_tools = 0
    62→    has_error = False
    63→
    64→    for step in window:
    65→        for tc in step.tool_calls:
    66→            cat = TOOL_CATEGORY_MAP.get(tc.function_name, "other")
    67→            category_counts[cat] = category_counts.get(cat, 0) + 1
    68→            total_tools += 1
    69→        # Check observation results for errors via content prefix convention
    70→        if step.observation:
    71→            for result in step.observation.results:
    72→                if is_error_content(result.content):
    73→                    has_error = True
    74→
    75→    if total_tools == 0:
    76→        return SessionPhase.PLANNING
    77→
    78→    read_count = category_counts.get("file_read", 0) + category_counts.get("search", 0)
    79→    write_count = category_counts.get("file_write", 0)
    80→    shell_count = category_counts.get("shell", 0)
    81→
    82→    read_ratio = read_count / total_tools
    83→    write_ratio = write_count / total_tools
    84→    shell_ratio = shell_count / total_tools
    85→
    86→    # Priority order reflects how agents typically work: errors with file
    87→    # activity signal debugging; shell-only (no writes, no errors) means
    88→    # running tests or checks; writes mean implementation; reads-only
    89→    # means exploring/understanding code before acting.
    90→    if has_error and (read_ratio + write_ratio) >= _DOMINANCE_THRESHOLD:
    91→        return SessionPhase.DEBUGGING
    92→    if shell_ratio >= 0.5 and write_count == 0 and not has_error:
    93→        return SessionPhase.VERIFICATION
    94→    if write_ratio >= _DOMINANCE_THRESHOLD:
    95→        return SessionPhase.IMPLEMENTATION
    96→    if read_ratio >= 0.6:
    97→        return SessionPhase.EXPLORATION
    98→
    99→    return SessionPhase.MIXED
   100→
   101→
   102→def _merge_adjacent(
   103→    window_phases: list[tuple[int, SessionPhase]], messages: list[Step]
   104→) -> list[PhaseSegment]:
   105→    """Merge adjacent windows with the same phase into segments."""
   106→    if not window_phases:
   107→        return []
   108→
   109→    segments: list[PhaseSegment] = []
   110→    current_phase = window_phases[0][1]
   111→    current_start = 0
   112→
   113→    for idx in range(1, len(window_phases)):
   114→        _, phase = window_phases[idx]
   115→        if phase != current_phase:
   116→            segments.append(_make_segment(messages, current_start, idx - 1, current_phase))
   117→            current_start = idx
   118→            current_phase = phase
   119→
   120→    segments.append(_make_segment(messages, current_start, len(messages) - 1, current_phase))
   121→    return segments
   122→
   123→
   124→def _absorb_small_segments(segments: list[PhaseSegment]) -> list[PhaseSegment]:
   125→    """Absorb sub-threshold segments (1 message) into their neighbors.
   126→
   127→    Merges backwards (into predecessor) rather than forwards so that a
   128→    single-message blip doesn't prematurely start a new phase trend.
   129→    """
   130→    if len(segments) <= 1:
   131→        return segments
   132→
   133→    result: list[PhaseSegment] = []
   134→    for seg in segments:
   135→        span = seg.end_index - seg.start_index + 1
   136→        if span <= 1 and result:
   137→            prev = result[-1]
   138→            result[-1] = PhaseSegment(
   139→                phase=prev.phase,
   140→                start_index=prev.start_index,
   141→                end_index=seg.end_index,
   142→                start_time=prev.start_time,
   143→                end_time=seg.end_time,
   144→                dominant_tool_category=prev.dominant_tool_category,
   145→                tool_call_count=prev.tool_call_count + seg.tool_call_count,
   146→            )
   147→        else:
   148→            result.append(seg)
   149→    return result
   150→
   151→
   152→def _make_segment(steps: list[Step], start: int, end: int, phase: SessionPhase) -> PhaseSegment:
   153→    """Build a PhaseSegment from a step range."""
   154→    segment_steps = steps[start : end + 1]
   155→    category_counts: dict[str, int] = {}
   156→    tool_count = 0
   157→    for step in segment_steps:
   158→        for tc in step.tool_calls:
   159→            cat = TOOL_CATEGORY_MAP.get(tc.function_name, "other")
   160→            category_counts[cat] = category_counts.get(cat, 0) + 1
   161→            tool_count += 1
   162→
   163→    dominant = max(category_counts, key=category_counts.get) if category_counts else ""
   164→    start_time = steps[start].timestamp if start < len(steps) else None
   165→    end_time = steps[end].timestamp if end < len(steps) else None
   166→
   167→    return PhaseSegment(
   168→        phase=phase,
   169→        start_index=start,
   170→        end_index=end,
   171→        start_time=start_time,
   172→        end_time=end_time,
   173→        dominant_tool_category=dominant,
   174→        tool_call_count=tool_count,
   175→    )
   176→

1→"""Step model for ATIF trajectories."""
     2→
     3→from datetime import datetime
     4→from typing import Any
     5→
     6→from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, model_validator
     7→
     8→from vibelens.models.enums import StepSource
     9→from vibelens.models.trajectories.content import ContentPart
    10→from vibelens.models.trajectories.metrics import Metrics
    11→from vibelens.models.trajectories.observation import Observation
    12→from vibelens.models.trajectories.tool_call import ToolCall
    13→from vibelens.utils.log import get_logger
    14→
    15→logger = get_logger(__name__)
    16→
    17→
    18→class Step(BaseModel):
    19→    """Unified step model (ATIF v1.6 compatible superset).
    20→
    21→    Field order follows Harbor reference implementation:
    22→    step_id, timestamp, source, model_name, reasoning_effort,
    23→    message, reasoning_content, tool_calls, observation, metrics,
    24→    is_copied_context, extra.
    25→    """
    26→
    27→    step_id: str = Field(
    28→        description=(
    29→            "Step identifier. ATIF uses ordinal int; VibeLens uses "
    30→            "UUID strings for stable cross-session referencing."
    31→        )
    32→    )
    33→    timestamp: datetime | None = Field(default=None, description="When the step was created.")
    34→    source: StepSource = Field(description="The originator of this step.")
    35→    model_name: str | None = Field(default=None, description="LLM model identifier for this step.")
    36→    reasoning_effort: str | float | None = Field(
    37→        default=None,
    38→        description="Qualitative or quantitative measure of reasoning effort (ATIF v1.6).",
    39→    )
    40→    message: str | list[ContentPart] = Field(
    41→        default="",
    42→        description=(
    43→            "Dialogue message. String for text-only, or ContentPart "
    44→            "array for multimodal content (ATIF v1.6)."
    45→        ),
    46→    )
    47→    reasoning_content: str | None = Field(
    48→        default=None, description="Agent's explicit internal reasoning text."
    49→    )
    50→    tool_calls: list[ToolCall] = Field(
    51→        default_factory=list,
    52→        description="Tool invocations extracted from this step.",
    53→    )
    54→    observation: Observation | None = Field(
    55→        default=None,
    56→        description="Tool execution results observed after this step.",
    57→    )
    58→    metrics: Metrics | None = Field(
    59→        default=None,
    60→        description="Token usage and cost statistics for this step.",
    61→    )
    62→    is_copied_context: bool | None = Field(
    63→        default=None,
    64→        description=(
    65→            "Whether this step was copied from a previous trajectory "
    66→            "for context during continuation (ATIF v1.5)."
    67→        ),
    68→    )
    69→    extra: dict[str, Any] | None = Field(default=None, description="Custom step-level metadata.")
    70→
    71→    @model_validator(mode="after")
    72→    def validate_tool_observation_pairing(self) -> "Step":
    73→        """Validate that tool_calls and observation results are paired.
    74→
    75→        - Orphaned observations (obs referencing non-existent tool_call)
    76→          raise ValueError — this is always a data integrity error.
    77→        - Orphaned tool_calls (tool_call with no matching observation)
    78→          log a warning — the last tool call in a session may not have
    79→          received its result yet.
    80→        """
    81→        if not self.tool_calls or not self.observation:
    82→            return self
    83→        tc_ids = {tc.tool_call_id for tc in self.tool_calls if tc.tool_call_id}
    84→        obs_ids = {r.source_call_id for r in self.observation.results if r.source_call_id}
    85→
    86→        orphaned_obs = obs_ids - tc_ids
    87→        if orphaned_obs:
    88→            raise ValueError(
    89→                f"Step {self.step_id}: observation(s) reference non-existent "
    90→                f"tool_call IDs: {orphaned_obs}"
    91→            )
    92→
    93→        orphaned_tc = tc_ids - obs_ids
    94→        if orphaned_tc:
    95→            logger.warning(
    96→                "Step %s: tool_call(s) without matching observation: %s",
    97→                self.step_id,
    98→                orphaned_tc,
    99→            )
   100→        return self
   101→

1→"""Tool call model for ATIF trajectories."""
     2→
     3→from typing import Any
     4→
     5→from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
     6→
     7→
     8→class ToolCall(BaseModel):
     9→    """Single tool call record (ATIF v1.6 compatible)."""
    10→
    11→    tool_call_id: str = Field(
    12→        default="", description="Unique tool-use identifier for pairing with observation results."
    13→    )
    14→    function_name: str = Field(
    15→        description="Name of the invoked tool (e.g. 'Bash', 'Read', 'Edit').",
    16→    )
    17→    arguments: dict | str | None = Field(default=None, description="Arguments passed to the tool.")
    18→    extra: dict[str, Any] | None = Field(
    19→        default=None, description="Custom tool call metadata (e.g. summary, output_digest)."
    20→    )
    21→

1→"""Per-session detailed analytics computation.
     2→
     3→Computes token breakdowns, tool frequency, step counts, phase segments,
     4→and cost estimation for a single session (main trajectory + sub-agents).
     5→"""
     6→
     7→from collections import defaultdict
     8→
     9→from vibelens.analysis.phase_detector import detect_phases
    10→from vibelens.analysis.pricing import compute_trajectory_cost
    11→from vibelens.models.analysis.dashboard import SessionAnalytics
    12→from vibelens.models.trajectories import Trajectory
    13→
    14→
    15→def compute_session_analytics(trajectories: list[Trajectory]) -> SessionAnalytics:
    16→    """Compute detailed analytics for a single session.
    17→
    18→    Args:
    19→        trajectories: Trajectory group for one session (main + sub-agents).
    20→
    21→    Returns:
    22→        SessionAnalytics with token breakdown, tool frequency, and phase segments.
    23→    """
    24→    if not trajectories:
    25→        raise ValueError("No trajectories provided for session analytics")
    26→
    27→    main = trajectories[0]
    28→    all_steps = []
    29→    for traj in trajectories:
    30→        all_steps.extend(traj.steps)
    31→
    32→    total_cost = 0.0
    33→    has_cost = False
    34→    for traj in trajectories:
    35→        traj_cost = compute_trajectory_cost(traj)
    36→        if traj_cost is not None:
    37→            total_cost += traj_cost
    38→            has_cost = True
    39→
    40→    return SessionAnalytics(
    41→        session_id=main.session_id,
    42→        token_breakdown=_compute_token_breakdown(trajectories),
    43→        tool_frequency=_compute_tool_frequency(trajectories),
    44→        step_count_by_source=_compute_step_counts(trajectories),
    45→        phase_segments=detect_phases(all_steps),
    46→        cost_usd=round(total_cost, 6) if has_cost else None,
    47→    )
    48→
    49→
    50→def _compute_token_breakdown(trajectories: list[Trajectory]) -> dict[str, int]:
    51→    """Aggregate token counts by category across all trajectories."""
    52→    prompt = 0
    53→    completion = 0
    54→    cache_read = 0
    55→    cache_write = 0
    56→
    57→    for traj in trajectories:
    58→        for step in traj.steps:
    59→            if step.metrics:
    60→                prompt += step.metrics.prompt_tokens
    61→                completion += step.metrics.completion_tokens
    62→                cache_read += step.metrics.cached_tokens
    63→                cache_write += step.metrics.cache_creation_tokens
    64→
    65→    return {
    66→        "prompt": prompt,
    67→        "completion": completion,
    68→        "cache_read": cache_read,
    69→        "cache_write": cache_write,
    70→    }
    71→
    72→
    73→def _compute_tool_frequency(trajectories: list[Trajectory]) -> dict[str, int]:
    74→    """Count tool calls by function name across all trajectories."""
    75→    counts: dict[str, int] = defaultdict(int)
    76→    for traj in trajectories:
    77→        for step in traj.steps:
    78→            for tc in step.tool_calls:
    79→                counts[tc.function_name] += 1
    80→    return dict(sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True))
    81→
    82→
    83→def _compute_step_counts(trajectories: list[Trajectory]) -> dict[str, int]:
    84→    """Count steps by source (user/agent/system) across all trajectories."""
    85→    counts: dict[str, int] = defaultdict(int)
    86→    for traj in trajectories:
    87→        for step in traj.steps:
    88→            counts[step.source.value] += 1
    89→    return dict(counts)
    90→

1→"""Dashboard aggregate analysis models."""
     2→
     3→from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
     4→
     5→from vibelens.models.analysis.phase import PhaseSegment
     6→
     7→
     8→class DailyStat(BaseModel):
     9→    """Single day aggregation for time series charts."""
    10→
    11→    date: str = Field(description="ISO date string (YYYY-MM-DD).")
    12→    session_count: int = Field(description="Number of sessions started on this day.")
    13→    total_messages: int = Field(default=0, description="Total messages on this day.")
    14→    total_tokens: int = Field(description="Sum of prompt + completion tokens.")
    15→    total_duration: int = Field(description="Sum of session durations in seconds.")
    16→    total_duration_hours: float = Field(description="Duration in hours (for chart Y-axis).")
    17→    total_cost_usd: float = Field(default=0.0, description="Estimated cost in USD for this day.")
    18→
    19→
    20→class PeriodStats(BaseModel):
    21→    """Aggregated metrics for a time period (month/week)."""
    22→
    23→    sessions: int = Field(default=0, description="Session count in this period.")
    24→    messages: int = Field(default=0, description="Message count in this period.")
    25→    tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Total tokens in this period.")
    26→    tool_calls: int = Field(default=0, description="Tool call count in this period.")
    27→    duration: int = Field(default=0, description="Duration in seconds in this period.")
    28→    input_tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Prompt/input tokens in this period.")
    29→    output_tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Completion/output tokens in this period.")
    30→    cache_read_tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Cache read tokens in this period.")
    31→    cache_creation_tokens: int = Field(
    32→        default=0, description="Cache creation tokens in this period."
    33→    )
    34→    cost_usd: float = Field(default=0.0, description="Estimated cost in USD for this period.")
    35→
    36→
    37→class DashboardStats(BaseModel):
    38→    """Aggregate statistics for the analysis dashboard.
    39→
    40→    Computed from full trajectory data via single-pass aggregation.
    41→    All time-dimension charts (daily_stats, hourly_distribution, heatmap)
    42→    exclude sessions with no timestamp.
    43→    """
    44→
    45→    total_sessions: int = Field(description="Total number of sessions.")
    46→    total_messages: int = Field(description="Total message count across all sessions.")
    47→    total_tokens: int = Field(description="Total tokens (prompt + completion).")
    48→    total_tool_calls: int = Field(description="Total tool invocations.")
    49→    total_duration: int = Field(description="Total duration in seconds.")
    50→    total_duration_hours: float = Field(description="Total duration in hours (display).")
    51→
    52→    # Token breakdown
    53→    total_input_tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Total prompt/input tokens.")
    54→    total_output_tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Total completion/output tokens.")
    55→    total_cache_tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Total cache read + write tokens.")
    56→    total_cache_read_tokens: int = Field(default=0, description="Total cache read tokens.")
    57→    total_cache_creation_tokens: int = Field(
    58→        default=0, description="Total cache creation/write tokens."
    59→    )
    60→
    61→    # Period breakdowns
    62→    this_year: PeriodStats = Field(default_factory=PeriodStats, description="Current year stats.")
    63→    this_month: PeriodStats = Field(default_factory=PeriodStats, description="Current month stats.")
    64→    this_week: PeriodStats = Field(default_factory=PeriodStats, description="Current week stats.")
    65→
    66→    # Averages
    67→    avg_messages_per_session: float = Field(default=0.0, description="Mean messages per session.")
    68→    avg_tokens_per_session: float = Field(default=0.0, description="Mean tokens per session.")
    69→    avg_tool_calls_per_session: float = Field(
    70→        default=0.0, description="Mean tool calls per session."
    71→    )
    72→    avg_duration_per_session: float = Field(
    73→        default=0.0, description="Mean duration per session (seconds)."
    74→    )
    75→
    76→    # Cost estimation
    77→    total_cost_usd: float = Field(default=0.0, description="Estimated total cost in USD.")
    78→    cost_by_model: dict[str, float] = Field(
    79→        default_factory=dict, description="Cost breakdown by canonical model name."
    80→    )
    81→    avg_cost_per_session: float = Field(
    82→        default=0.0, description="Mean estimated cost per session in USD."
    83→    )
    84→
    85→    # Project count
    86→    project_count: int = Field(default=0, description="Number of unique projects.")
    87→
    88→    # Daily activity heatmap (YYYY-MM-DD -> session count)
    89→    daily_activity: dict[str, int] = Field(
    90→        default_factory=dict, description="Session count per day for yearly heatmap."
    91→    )
    92→
    93→    daily_stats: list[DailyStat] = Field(description="Per-day aggregations for time series.")
    94→    model_distribution: dict[str, int] = Field(description="Session count keyed by model name.")
    95→    project_distribution: dict[str, int] = Field(description="Session count keyed by project path.")
    96→    hourly_distribution: dict[int, int] = Field(
    97→        description="Session starts keyed by hour of day (0-23)."
    98→    )
    99→    weekday_hour_heatmap: dict[str, int] = Field(
   100→        description="Session starts keyed by 'weekday_hour' (e.g. '1_14' = Monday 14:00)."
   101→    )
   102→    agent_distribution: dict[str, int] = Field(
   103→        default_factory=dict,
   104→        description="Session count keyed by agent name (e.g. claude-code, codex, gemini).",
   105→    )
   106→    timezone: str = Field(default="UTC", description="IANA timezone name used for time groupings.")
   107→
   108→
   109→class SessionAnalytics(BaseModel):
   110→    """Per-session analytics for the session detail view."""
   111→
   112→    session_id: str = Field(description="Session identifier.")
   113→    token_breakdown: dict[str, int] = Field(
   114→        description="Token counts by category: prompt, completion, cache_read, cache_write."
   115→    )
   116→    tool_frequency: dict[str, int] = Field(description="Tool call counts keyed by function name.")
   117→    step_count_by_source: dict[str, int] = Field(
   118→        description="Step counts keyed by source: user, agent, system."
   119→    )
   120→    phase_segments: list[PhaseSegment] = Field(
   121→        description="Conversation phase segments from PhaseDetector."
   122→    )
   123→    cost_usd: float | None = Field(
   124→        default=None, description="Estimated session cost in USD based on API pricing."
   125→    )
   126→

1→"""Phase segment model for conversation phase detection results."""
     2→
     3→from datetime import datetime
     4→
     5→from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
     6→
     7→from vibelens.models.enums import SessionPhase

     9→
    10→class PhaseSegment(BaseModel):
    11→    """A contiguous range of steps sharing the same phase."""
    12→
    13→    phase: SessionPhase = Field(description="Detected phase for this segment.")
    14→    start_index: int = Field(description="First message index (inclusive).")

    16→    start_time: datetime | None = Field(default=None, description="Timestamp of first message.")
    17→    end_time: datetime | None = Field(default=None, description="Timestamp of last message.")
    18→    dominant_tool_category: str = Field(
    19→        default="", description="Most frequent tool category in this segment."
    20→    )
    21→    tool_call_count: int = Field(default=0, description="Total tool calls in this segment.")

    25→    limit: int = 500,

    45→    q: str = "", sources: str = "user_prompts", x_session_token: str | None = Header(None)
    46→) -> list[str]:
    47→    """Search sessions by query across selected text sources.
    48→
    49→    Args:

    56→    """
    57→    if not q:

    60→    return search_sessions(q, source_list, session_token=x_session_token)

    99→    payload = [t.model_dump(mode="json") for t in group]

Now I have enough information to provide a comprehensive analysis. Let me compile this into a clear report: ## VibeLens Codebase Analysis: Conversation Flow Diagrams Based on a thorough exploration of the VibeLens codebase, here's what's available for implementing conversation flow diagrams: --- ### 1. Backend Data Models & Analysis #### **ToolDependencyGraph Model** (`tool_graph.py`) ```python class ToolEdge(BaseModel): source_tool_call_id: str target_tool_call_id: str relation: str # "read_before_write" | "error_retry" | "search_then_read" | "sequential" shared_resource: str # file path or pattern class ToolDependencyGraph(BaseModel): session_id: str nodes: list[str] # Tool call IDs in topological order edges: list[ToolEdge] root_nodes: list[str] # Starting points with no predecessors ``` **Key insight**: The backend already infers causal relationships between tool calls (read→edit chains, error→retry loops, search→read flows). The DAG is ready to visualize—you just need to expose it via API. #### **Step Model** (`step.py`) ```python class Step(BaseModel): step_id: str timestamp: datetime | None source: StepSource # "user" | "agent" | "system" model_name: str | None message: str | list[ContentPart] tool_calls: list[ToolCall] observation: Observation | None ``` Each step contains: - **tool_calls**: Extracted function invocations with IDs and arguments - **observation**: Results mapped to tool_call_id - **source**: Identifies user prompts (→), agent responses (◆), or system messages #### **ToolCall Model** (`tool_call.py`) ```python class ToolCall(BaseModel): tool_call_id: str # UUID for pairing with observation function_name: str # "Read", "Edit", "Bash", "Glob", "Grep", etc. arguments: dict | str | None ``` #### **Phase Detection** (`phase_detector.py`) Already categorizes windows of tool calls into: - EXPLORATION (reads/searches dominate) - IMPLEMENTATION (file writes) - DEBUGGING (errors + file activity) - VERIFICATION (shell-only, no writes) - PLANNING (no tools) This provides semantic context for coloring flow diagram nodes. #### **Tool Category Mapping** (`constants.py`) ```python TOOL_CATEGORY_MAP = { "Read": "file_read", "Edit"/"Write": "file_write", "Bash": "shell", "Glob"/"Grep": "search", "WebSearch"/"WebFetch": "web", "Agent"/"Task"/"Skill": "agent", } ``` This semantic categorization is essential for visual encoding (colors, icons, grouping). --- ### 2. Frontend Type System #### **Step Type** (`types.ts`) ```typescript interface Step { step_id: string timestamp?: string | null source: "user" | "agent" | "system" model_name?: string | null message: string | ContentPart[] tool_calls: ToolCall[] observation?: Observation | null metrics?: Metrics | null } interface ToolCall { tool_call_id: string function_name: string arguments: unknown } ``` #### **Tool Type & Colors** (`types.ts`) ```typescript export type ToolType = "bash" | "edit" | "read" | "search" | "communication" | "task" | "think" | "other" export const TOOL_TYPE_COLORS: Record = { bash: "bg-yellow-400", edit: "bg-green-500", read: "bg-blue-600", search: "bg-sky-300", communication: "bg-orange-400", task: "bg-purple-400", think: "bg-gray-400", other: "bg-gray-300", } ``` Color palette is already established and consistent. --- ### 3. Frontend Current Architecture #### **Session View Layout** (`session-view.tsx`) - Two-column design: main content + sidebar - **Left**: Steps rendered via `StepTimeline` (vertical timeline with dots and connectors) - **Right**: `PromptNavPanel` (collapsible navigation sidebar) - Resizable dividers with smooth drag **Pattern to reuse**: The timeline visualization structure with dots, connectors, and event metadata. #### **StepTimeline Component** (`step-timeline.tsx`) ```typescript interface TimelineEntry { step: Step content: ReactNode // Rendered step block } // Renders vertical timeline with: // - Colored dots (user=indigo, agent=cyan, system=zinc, skill=amber) // - Vertical connectors // - Time badges (elapsed + wall-clock) // - Arbitrary content in each entry ``` **Reusable pattern**: This provides the basic timeline rails. Flow diagrams can overlay SVG layers on this. #### **Dashboard View Structure** (`dashboard-view.tsx`) - Grid layout with stat cards (6-column grid) - Rounded bordered sections (`rounded-xl border border-zinc-700/60 bg-zinc-900/80`) - Tooltip system for hover context - Consistent spacing/typography --- ### 4. Existing SVG Visualization Components #### **Activity Heatmap** (`activity-heatmap.tsx`) ```typescript // SVG with: // - Dynamic width/height calculated from data // - Rect elements with hover interactivity // - Month/day labels positioned in SVG // - Tooltip integration via onHover callbacks // - Color mapping: getHeatmapColor(count, maxVal) → RGBA // - Responsive viewBox pattern const width = LABEL_WIDTH + HEATMAP_WEEKS * (CELL_SIZE + CELL_GAP) + 10 const height = HEADER_HEIGHT + 7 * (CELL_SIZE + CELL_GAP) + 10 ``` #### **Usage Over Time Chart** (`usage-over-time-chart.tsx`) ```typescript // Advanced SVG with: // - Line + area chart (path geometry) // - Grid lines with Y-axis labels // - Data point circles (r changes on hover) // - Vertical crosshair on hover (dashed line) // - Invisible overlay rect for mouse tracking // - formatY() mapper for different metrics // - Responsive scaling with viewBox const W = 800, H = 200 const ML = 55, MR = 15, MT = 12, MB = 28 // Margins const PW = W - ML - MR, PH = H - MT - MB // Plot area ``` #### **BarRow Component** (`bar-row.tsx`) ```typescript // Gradient bar with: // - Tailwind gradients (from-cyan-600 to-cyan-400) // - Percentage-based width scaling // - Hover state (group-hover changes) // - Tooltip on hover ``` #### **Chart Utils** (`chart-utils.ts`) ```typescript export function fillDateGaps(data: DailyStat[]): DailyStat[] // Interpolate missing dates export function groupDailyStats(data: DailyStat[], group: TimeGroup): DailyStat[] export function getHeatmapColor(count: number, maxVal: number): string ``` #### **Chart Constants** (`chart-constants.ts`) ```typescript export const HEATMAP_WEEKS = 52 export const CELL_SIZE = 13 export const CELL_GAP = 3 export const LABEL_WIDTH = 28 export const HEADER_HEIGHT = 20 export const TOOLTIP_OFFSET = 12 export const HEATMAP_COLORS = [ "rgba(34,211,238,0.06)", // Transparent cyan "rgba(34,211,238,0.25)", "rgba(34,211,238,0.45)", "rgba(34,211,238,0.65)", "rgba(34,211,238,0.85)", // Solid cyan ] export const MODEL_COLORS = [ "bg-blue-500", "bg-amber-400", "bg-rose-500", "bg-emerald-500", "bg-violet-500", "bg-orange-500", "bg-cyan-400", "bg-fuchsia-500", ] ``` --- ### 5. API Endpoints Available #### **Session Data** ``` GET /api/sessions/{session_id} Returns: list[Trajectory] with full steps, tool_calls, observations ``` #### **Session Analytics** ``` GET /api/analysis/sessions/{session_id}/stats Returns: SessionAnalytics { session_id, token_breakdown, tool_frequency, step_count_by_source, phase_segments, cost_usd } ``` The **tool_frequency** dict can be enriched to include ToolDependencyGraph if you expose a new endpoint. --- ### 6. Design Patterns to Reuse #### **Tooltip System** ```typescript // From tooltip.tsx & dashboard-view.tsx const { tip, show, move, hide } = useTooltip() show(e, "tooltip text\nmultiline")} onMouseMove={move} onMouseLeave={hide} /> ``` #### **Dark Theme Color Palette** ``` Backgrounds: bg-zinc-950, bg-zinc-900, bg-zinc-900/80 Borders: border-zinc-800, border-zinc-700/50 Text: text-zinc-100, text-zinc-300, text-zinc-500 Highlights: text-cyan-400, text-amber-300, text-emerald-400 ``` #### **Tailwind Sizing** - Components use `rounded-xl` (xl = 0.75rem) - Padding: `p-5` (1.25rem), `px-4 py-2` combos - Responsive via grid columns and gap scaling --- ### 7. Critical Integration Points #### **Where to Add Flow Diagram** In **session-view.tsx**, the structure is: ```tsx
...
``` **Option A**: Add a third column between steps and nav (left-middle-right layout) **Option B**: Add as a tab/toggle in the header above steps **Option C**: Add as an overlay/modal that appears on click #### **Data Flow for Tool Graph** Currently: 1. Session loads via `GET /api/sessions/{session_id}` → Trajectory[] 2. StepTimeline iterates over steps and renders To add flow diagrams: 1. Add backend endpoint: `GET /api/analysis/sessions/{session_id}/tool-graph` 2. Returns: ToolDependencyGraph with nodes (tool_call IDs) and edges (relations) 3. Map tool_call IDs → step indices for coordination 4. Fetch in parallel with session analytics (non-blocking) --- ### 8. Data Shape Summary for Implementation **To render a flow diagram, you need**: ```typescript interface FlowDiagramData { // Nodes: each represents a tool call toolCalls: Array<{ id: string // tool_call_id stepIndex: number // which step contains it functionName: string // "Read", "Edit", "Bash", etc. category: "file_read" | "file_write" | "shell" | "search" | "web" | "agent" arguments?: Record timestamp?: string // from step output?: string // from observation }> // Edges: causal relationships edges: Array<{ source: string // tool_call_id target: string // tool_call_id relation: "read_before_write" | "error_retry" | "search_then_read" | "sequential" label?: string // e.g., shared file path }> // Phase context phases: Array<{ startIndex: number endIndex: number phase: "exploration" | "implementation" | "debugging" | "verification" | "planning" | "mixed" }> } ``` --- ### 9. Key Takeaways for Implementation 1. **Backend is 80% ready**: `ToolDependencyGraph` exists with proper edge detection logic. Just expose it via API. 2. **SVG patterns established**: UsageOverTimeChart shows how to: - Calculate viewBox dimensions dynamically - Handle hover interactivity with invisible overlay - Use gradients and dashed lines - Integrate tooltip system 3. **Color/type system ready**: Tool categories map to a semantic taxonomy (file_read, file_write, shell, search, etc.) 4. **Timeline UI exists**: StepTimeline provides vertical layout with dots & connectors—flow diagram can follow this aesthetic. 5. **Dark theme consistent**: All components use `zinc-*` and cyan/amber accents. 6. **Tooltip system proven**: Multi-line tooltips with mouse tracking work well (used in heatmap and charts). --- ### Files to Reference When Building: - **Backend**: `/vibelens/analysis/tool_graph.py`, `/vibelens/analysis/phase_detector.py` - **Frontend types**: `/frontend/src/types.ts` - **SVG patterns**: `/frontend/src/components/analysis/usage-over-time-chart.tsx`, `/activity-heatmap.tsx` - **Timeline pattern**: `/frontend/src/components/conversation/step-timeline.tsx` - **Tooltip system**: `/frontend/src/components/analysis/tooltip.tsx`, `dashboard-view.tsx` - **Styling**: All components use Tailwind with established `rounded-xl border border-zinc-700/60 bg-zinc-900/80` card pattern

Existing SVG Visualization Components #### **Activity Heatmap** (`activity-heatmap.tsx`) ```typescript // SVG with: // - Dynamic width/height calculated from data // - Rect elements with hover interactivity // - Month/day labels positioned in SVG // - Tooltip integration via onHover callbacks // - Color mapping: getHeatmapColor(count, maxVal) → RGBA // - Responsive viewBox pattern const width = LABEL_WIDTH + HEATMAP_WEEKS * (CELL_SIZE + CELL_GAP) + 10 const height = HEADER_HEIGHT + 7 * (CELL_SIZE + CELL_GAP) + 10 ``` #### **Usage Over Time Chart** (`usage-over-time-chart.tsx`) ```typescript // Advanced SVG with: // - Line + area chart (path geometry) // - Grid lines with Y-axis labels // - Data point circles (r changes on hover) // - Vertical crosshair on hover (dashed line) // - Invisible overlay rect for mouse tracking // - formatY() mapper for different metrics // - Responsive scaling with viewBox const W = 800, H = 200 const ML = 55, MR = 15, MT = 12, MB = 28 // Margins const PW = W - ML - MR, PH = H - MT - MB // Plot area ``` #### **BarRow Component** (`bar-row.tsx`) ```typescript // Gradient bar with: // - Tailwind gradients (from-cyan-600 to-cyan-400) // - Percentage-based width scaling // - Hover state (group-hover changes) // - Tooltip on hover ``` #### **Chart Utils** (`chart-utils.ts`) ```typescript export function fillDateGaps(data: DailyStat[]): DailyStat[] // Interpolate missing dates export function groupDailyStats(data: DailyStat[], group: TimeGroup): DailyStat[] export function getHeatmapColor(count: number, maxVal: number): string ``` #### **Chart Constants** (`chart-constants.ts`) ```typescript export const HEATMAP_WEEKS = 52 export const CELL_SIZE = 13 export const CELL_GAP = 3 export const LABEL_WIDTH = 28 export const HEADER_HEIGHT = 20 export const TOOLTIP_OFFSET = 12 export const HEATMAP_COLORS = [ "rgba(34,211,238,0.06)", // Transparent cyan "rgba(34,211,238,0.25)", "rgba(34,211,238,0.45)", "rgba(34,211,238,0.65)", "rgba(34,211,238,0.85)", // Solid cyan ] export const MODEL_COLORS = [ "bg-blue-500", "bg-amber-400", "bg-rose-500", "bg-emerald-500", "bg-violet-500", "bg-orange-500", "bg-cyan-400", "bg-fuchsia-500", ] ``` --- ### 5. API Endpoints Available #### **Session Data** ``` GET /api/sessions/{session_id} Returns: list[Trajectory] with full steps, tool_calls, observations ``` #### **Session Analytics** ``` GET /api/analysis/sessions/{session_id}/stats Returns: SessionAnalytics { session_id, token_breakdown, tool_frequency, step_count_by_source, phase_segments, cost_usd } ``` The **tool_frequency** dict can be enriched to include ToolDependencyGraph if you expose a new endpoint. --- ### 6. Design Patterns to Reuse #### **Tooltip System** ```typescript // From tooltip.tsx & dashboard-view.tsx const { tip, show, move, hide } = useTooltip() show(e, "tooltip text\nmultiline")} onMouseMove={move} onMouseLeave={hide} /> ``` #### **Dark Theme Color Palette** ``` Backgrounds: bg-zinc-950, bg-zinc-900, bg-zinc-900/80 Borders: border-zinc-800, border-zinc-700/50 Text: text-zinc-100, text-zinc-300, text-zinc-500 Highlights: text-cyan-400, text-amber-300, text-emerald-400 ``` #### **Tailwind Sizing** - Components use `rounded-xl` (xl = 0.75rem) - Padding: `p-5` (1.25rem), `px-4 py-2` combos - Responsive via grid columns and gap scaling --- ### 7. Critical Integration Points #### **Where to Add Flow Diagram** In **session-view.tsx**, the structure is: ```tsx
...
``` **Option A**: Add a third column between steps and nav (left-middle-right layout) **Option B**: Add as a tab/toggle in the header above steps **Option C**: Add as an overlay/modal that appears on click #### **Data Flow for Tool Graph** Currently: 1. Session loads via `GET /api/sessions/{session_id}` → Trajectory[] 2. StepTimeline iterates over steps and renders To add flow diagrams: 1. Add backend endpoint: `GET /api/analysis/sessions/{session_id}/tool-graph` 2. Returns: ToolDependencyGraph with nodes (tool_call IDs) and edges (relations) 3. Map tool_call IDs → step indices for coordination 4. Fetch in parallel with session analytics (non-blocking) --- ### 8. Data Shape Summary for Implementation **To render a flow diagram, you need**: ```typescript interface FlowDiagramData { // Nodes: each represents a tool call toolCalls: Array<{ id: string // tool_call_id stepIndex: number // which step contains it functionName: string // "Read", "Edit", "Bash", etc. category: "file_read" | "file_write" | "shell" | "search" | "web" | "agent" arguments?: Record timestamp?: string // from step output?: string // from observation }> // Edges: causal relationships edges: Array<{ source: string // tool_call_id target: string // tool_call_id relation: "read_before_write" | "error_retry" | "search_then_read" | "sequential" label?: string // e.g., shared file path }> // Phase context phases: Array<{ startIndex: number endIndex: number phase: "exploration" | "implementation" | "debugging" | "verification" | "planning" | "mixed" }> } ``` --- ### 9. Key Takeaways for Implementation 1. **Backend is 80% ready**: `ToolDependencyGraph` exists with proper edge detection logic. Just expose it via API. 2. **SVG patterns established**: UsageOverTimeChart shows how to: - Calculate viewBox dimensions dynamically - Handle hover interactivity with invisible overlay - Use gradients and dashed lines - Integrate tooltip system 3. **Color/type system ready**: Tool categories map to a semantic taxonomy (file_read, file_write, shell, search, etc.) 4. **Timeline UI exists**: StepTimeline provides vertical layout with dots & connectors—flow diagram can follow this aesthetic. 5. **Dark theme consistent**: All components use `zinc-*` and cyan/amber accents. 6. **Tooltip system proven**: Multi-line tooltips with mouse tracking work well (used in heatmap and charts). --- ### Files to Reference When Building: - **Backend**: `/vibelens/analysis/tool_graph.py`, `/vibelens/analysis/phase_detector.py` - **Frontend types**: `/frontend/src/types.ts` - **SVG patterns**: `/frontend/src/components/analysis/usage-over-time-chart.tsx`, `/activity-heatmap.tsx` - **Timeline pattern**: `/frontend/src/components/conversation/step-timeline.tsx` - **Tooltip system**: `/frontend/src/components/analysis/tooltip.tsx`, `dashboard-view.tsx` - **Styling**: All components use Tailwind with established `rounded-xl border border-zinc-700/60 bg-zinc-900/80` card pattern

1→# VibeLens Roadmap v1 — Feasibility Report
     2→
     3→> Per-item feasibility assessment for all roadmap items. Each entry includes effort estimate, feasibility rating, population appeal, and rationale.
     4→
     5→| Item | Detail |
     6→|------|--------|
     7→| **Roadmap** | [`roadmap_v1.md`](roadmap_v1.md) |
     8→| **Date** | March 2025 |
     9→| **Scope** | 39 pending items across 10 categories |
    10→
    11→This report evaluates every item on the VibeLens v1 roadmap and documents the rationale for items that were removed. It serves two purposes: helping prioritize implementation order and providing a reference for contributors assessing the scope of individual features.
    12→
    13→## Rating Scale
    14→
    15→- **Effort**: Low (~1-2 days) / Medium (~3-7 days) / High (~2-4 weeks)
    16→- **Feasibility**: Straightforward / Moderate / Challenging
    17→- **Pop.** (Population): HIGH / MED / LOW — How many users would actually use this feature
    18→
    19→## Summary
    20→
    21→| Dimension | HIGH | MED | LOW |
    22→|-----------|------|-----|-----|
    23→| Population | 13 | 21 | 5 |
    24→
    25→| Feasibility | Count | Examples |
    26→|-------------|-------|----------|
    27→| Straightforward | 12 | Cost estimation, Cowork parser, virtual scrolling, zero-config launch |
    28→| Moderate | 25 | Session highlights, friction detection, session DAG, GitHub integration |
    29→| Challenging | 2 | Prompt effectiveness scoring, ATIF v2.0 spec |
    30→
    31→The sweet spot is **HIGH population + Straightforward feasibility** — 6 items fall in this category: cost estimation, Cowork parser, PyPI packaging, zero-config launch, local-first privacy, and shareable session links. These should be prioritized first.
    32→
    33→---
    34→
    35→## 1. Cost Tracking
    36→
    37→The existing `Metrics` model already captures per-step token counts (prompt, completion, cached, cache-creation), making most cost-related features a matter of aggregation and presentation rather than new data collection.
    38→
    39→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
    40→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
    41→| ~~Token breakdown~~ | — | Done | — | Already implemented in step metrics display. |
    42→| ~~Period comparisons~~ | — | Done | — | Date-range filtering exists in dashboard analytics. |
    43→| Cost estimation | Medium | Straightforward | HIGH | Token counts already exist in `Metrics`; need a pricing table per model and simple multiplication. Everyone using paid agents wants to see dollar amounts — this is the single most requested missing feature. |
    44→| Burn rate projection | Medium | Moderate | HIGH | "How much will I spend this month?" — universal question for budget-conscious users. Simple linear extrapolation, no ML. ~60% of users care about budgeting. |
    45→| Cost-per-task-type | Medium | Moderate | MED | Depends on phase detection (already done) to attribute tokens to phases. Useful for users optimizing spend, but most just want the total — maybe 30-40% of users would drill down to this level. |
    46→| Model specialization ROI | Medium | Moderate | MED | Requires grouping sessions by model, then comparing cost-normalized quality metrics. Appeals to multi-model users, but many stick to one model. Growing audience as more models launch. |
    47→
    48→## 2. Session Insights
    49→
    50→Inspired by Claude Code's `/insights` report, which surfaces wins, friction points, outcomes, and usage personality. These features bring the same kind of personalized analytics to VibeLens — telling users what went well, what went wrong, and how effective their sessions were.
    51→
    52→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
    53→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
    54→| ~~Phase detection~~ | — | Done | — | Sliding-window classifier implemented in `phase_detector.py`. |
    55→| ~~Tool dependency graph~~ | — | Done | — | DAG construction in `tool_graph.py`. |
    56→| Session highlights | Medium | Moderate | HIGH | Auto-identify wins/accomplishments from sessions. The "Spotify Wrapped" effect — everyone loves seeing their achievements surfaced. Highly shareable, drives engagement. ~70% of users would check this. |
    57→| Friction detection | Medium | Moderate | HIGH | Surface where sessions went wrong. "Where am I wasting time?" is universally interesting. Maps directly to `/insights` "Where Things Go Wrong" — proven to be the most engaging section. ~60% of users. |
    58→| Outcome scoring | Medium | Moderate | MED | Rate sessions by goal achievement. Interesting but somewhat abstract — users check occasionally rather than daily. ~40% of users would engage with this view. |
    59→| Error pattern recognition | Medium | Moderate | MED | Requires classifying tool call failures by error type. Useful for recurring problems, but most users don't have enough sessions to see statistical patterns. ~30% of active users. |
    60→| Rework detection | Medium | Moderate | MED | "Am I going in circles?" is relatable, but not top-of-mind. Appeals to users who review their sessions for process improvement. ~25% of users. |
    61→| Prompt effectiveness scoring | High | Challenging | LOW | Most users don't systematically optimize prompts. Defining "effectiveness" is subjective, and the scoring would need extensive validation. ~15% of power users at most. |
    62→
    63→## 3. Multi-Agent Analytics
    64→
    65→Both remaining items are medium effort. The concurrent session dashboard ([issue #2](https://github.com/user/VibeLens/issues/2)) can leverage the existing `correlator.py` infrastructure for time-window overlap detection.
    66→
    67→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
    68→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
    69→| ~~Sub-agent hierarchy~~ | — | Done | — | Recursive sub-agent display implemented. |
    70→| ~~Cross-agent correlation~~ | — | Done | — | Project-path grouping with time-window overlap in `correlator.py`. |
    71→| Agent scorecards | Medium | Moderate | MED | Aggregating per-agent metrics across sessions. Multi-model users love comparisons, but single-agent users (still the majority) get less value. ~30% of users. |
    72→| Concurrent session dashboard | Medium | Moderate | MED | "Multi-clauding" is a growing pattern (~7% of messages in `/insights` data) but still a minority workflow. Visually impressive in demos though. ~25% of users, growing. |
    73→
    74→## 4. Session Graphs
    75→
    76→This section focuses on session-level relationship visualizations built from `TrajectoryRef` cross-references. Low-appeal items (cross-session diff, 24-week activity board) were cut in favor of features with broader visual appeal.
    77→
    78→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
    79→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
    80→| ~~Activity heatmap~~ | — | Done | — | 52-week GitHub-style heatmap implemented. |
    81→| ~~Usage-over-time chart~~ | — | Done | — | Line chart for sessions/messages/tokens. |
    82→| Conversation flow diagrams | Medium | Moderate | HIGH | Visually stunning diagrams of user→agent→tool flows. The kind of screenshot that makes people want to try VibeLens. Great for README, blog posts, sharing. ~50% of users would explore this. |
    83→| Session relationship DAG | Medium | Moderate | MED | Visually impressive graph of session relationships. Great for demos and power users with complex workflows, but most users have simple linear session histories. ~25% of users. |
    84→| Session continuation chains | Low | Straightforward | MED | Users who frequently resume sessions want to see the chain. Simple to build, moderate appeal. ~30% of users who use session continuation. |
    85→
    86→## 5. Agent Parsers
    87→
    88→Cowork and OpenClaw are the highest-priority new parsers. Cowork is especially low-effort because it likely shares Claude Code's JSONL format and only needs a different discovery path. OpenClaw follows a similar JSONL pattern with its own directory structure at `~/.example-project/`.
    89→
    90→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
    91→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
    92→| ~~Claude Code parser~~ | — | Done | — | Full JSONL parsing with sub-agent discovery. |
    93→| ~~Codex CLI parser~~ | — | Done | — | OpenAI Responses API rollout format. |
    94→| ~~Gemini CLI parser~~ | — | Done | — | Session JSON with project path resolution. |
    95→| ~~Dataclaw parser~~ | — | Done | — | HuggingFace JSONL export ingestion. |
    96→| ~~Auto-detection~~ | — | Done | — | Confidence-scored format fingerprinting. |
    97→| Cowork parser | Low | Straightforward | HIGH | Anthropic's desktop agent has a large and growing user base. Supporting Cowork means VibeLens works for GUI users too, not just CLI power users. Potentially doubles the addressable audience. |
    98→| MCP server integration | Medium | Moderate | HIGH | MCP is the hottest standard in the agent ecosystem. Being an MCP server means users can query VibeLens analytics from inside their agent sessions — "meta-observability." Huge discoverability boost. |
    99→| OpenClaw parser | Medium | Straightforward | MED | Smaller community than Claude Code/Cowork, but an active open-source project. Each new parser incrementally expands the user base. |
   100→| OpenCode parser | Medium | Moderate | MED | Growing agent community. Less documentation available for reverse-engineering the format. |
   101→| ATIF v2.0 spec | High | Challenging | LOW | Internal schema infrastructure. Users never see this directly. Only matters to VibeLens contributors and integrators. Defer until v1 stabilizes. |
   102→
   103→## 6. Skill Coaching
   104→
   105→Reduced from 5 items to 3 by cutting vague features (skill extraction, session-based coaching) that depend on unbuilt prerequisites. The remaining items are self-contained and deliver value independently.
   106→
   107→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
   108→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
   109→| Productivity pattern library | Medium | Moderate | MED | Useful tips, but users may not seek productivity advice inside an analytics tool. Similar to `/insights` CLAUDE.md suggestions — helpful when surfaced, but not a destination feature. ~30% of users. |
   110→| Guided onboarding | Medium | Straightforward | MED | Only new users, and they may prefer docs/videos. One-time use per user, but reduces churn at the critical first-experience moment. ~25% of new users complete onboarding flows. |
   111→| Best practices dashboard | Medium | Moderate | MED | Interesting for teams and power users. Depends on outcome scoring to identify which sessions are "high quality." ~25% of users. |
   112→
   113→## 7. Packaging
   114→
   115→Driven by [issue #3](https://github.com/user/VibeLens/issues/3). All four items are straightforward to moderate — standard Python packaging concerns with no algorithmic risk. The main challenge is the CI/CD pipeline for automated PyPI releases with bundled frontend assets.
   116→
   117→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
   118→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
   119→| PyPI packaging | Medium | Straightforward | HIGH | The gateway to adoption. Without `pip install vibelens`, every potential user faces a git-clone-and-configure barrier. This is the single highest-leverage item on the entire roadmap. 100% of potential users benefit. |
   120→| Zero-config launch | Low | Straightforward | HIGH | Paired with PyPI packaging, this creates the magic "install and it works" moment. Auto-detecting `~/.claude/` eliminates the YAML config friction that kills first-time users. 100% of self-use users. |
   121→| Frontend asset bundling | Medium | Moderate | HIGH | Technical prerequisite for PyPI to work. Invisible to users but without it, `pip install` doesn't include the UI. 100% of PyPI users need this. |
   122→| Local-first privacy | Low | Straightforward | HIGH | Privacy is table stakes for a tool that reads your agent conversations. Binding to localhost by default is a trust signal that every user appreciates, even if they don't think about it explicitly. ~90% of users. |
   123→
   124→## 8. Privacy & Security
   125→
   126→Scope reduced from the original roadmap — contextual redaction and anomaly detection were removed as overengineered for current needs. The remaining items (sensitive data auditor, data retention) are practical and well-scoped.
   127→
   128→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
   129→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
   130→| ~~Upload isolation~~ | — | Done | — | Session-token scoped pipeline. |
   131→| Sensitive data auditor | Medium | Moderate | MED | Becomes important when users share sessions or export data. "Did I accidentally leak an API key?" is a real concern. ~30% of users, rising with shareable links. |
   132→| Data retention policies | Low | Straightforward | LOW | Nice-to-have for long-running installations. Most users don't accumulate enough sessions to need auto-cleanup. ~15% of power users on shared machines. |
   133→
   134→## 9. Performance
   135→
   136→Memory-mapped JSONL reading was removed (marginal gains, OS-specific complexity). The remaining items follow well-trodden paths — incremental parsing, SQLite FTS5, and virtual scrolling all have mature libraries and documented patterns.
   137→
   138→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
   139→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
   140→| ~~In-memory caching~~ | — | Done | — | TTL-based caching for aggregations. |
   141→| Incremental parsing | Medium | Moderate | HIGH | Directly impacts startup time. Users with 500+ sessions currently wait several seconds on each launch. Eliminating that wait makes VibeLens feel instant. ~50% of active users hit this pain point within months of use. |
   142→| SQLite FTS5 search | Medium | Moderate | MED | Better search with persistence across restarts. In-memory search works for most users today. Becomes important as session counts grow. ~30% of users search frequently enough to notice. |
   143→| Virtual scrolling | Low | Straightforward | MED | Only matters with 100+ sessions in the list. Standard optimization, near-zero risk. ~30% of users have enough sessions to benefit. |
   144→| File system watching | Medium | Moderate | MED | Live-reload is delightful — sessions appear as you use your agent without manual refresh. Nice polish but not critical. ~25% of users would notice and appreciate it. |
   145→| Lazy step loading | Medium | Moderate | MED | Only matters for very long sessions (100+ steps). Prevents UI freeze on massive conversations. ~25% of users have sessions long enough to benefit. |
   146→
   147→## 10. Sharing & Integration
   148→
   149→The new shareable session links feature is the most exciting addition — it lets users publicly share conversations, driving organic adoption. Jira/Linear sync was cut due to low demand and high bidirectional sync complexity.
   150→
   151→| Item | Effort | Feasibility | Pop. | Rationale |
   152→|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
   153→| Shareable session links | Medium | Straightforward | HIGH | Viral growth loop: users share interesting sessions → others see VibeLens in action → new users install it. This is the most important growth feature. Also useful for bug reports, code reviews, and team discussions. ~60% of users would share at least once. |
   154→| GitHub integration | High | Moderate | MED | Developer teams want session-to-commit linking. Individual hobbyists less so. GitHub API complexity (rate limits, auth) makes this a significant investment for moderate audience. ~30% of users. |
   155→| Slack / Discord bot | Medium | Moderate | MED | Team feature for sharing session summaries in channels. Individual users (the majority today) don't need this. ~20% of team users. |
   156→| Multi-format export | Medium | Straightforward | LOW | CSV and Parquet export for data analysts and researchers. Most users never need to export raw data — they use the UI. ~15% of users. |
   157→| HuggingFace export | Medium | Moderate | LOW | Academic researchers who want to publish anonymized datasets. Very small audience today, but important for VibeLens's credibility in the research community. ~10% of users. |
   158→
   159→---
   160→
   161→## Priority Matrix
   162→
   163→Items sorted by the combination of population appeal and implementation feasibility:
   164→
   165→### Tier 1: Do First (HIGH pop. + Straightforward)
   166→
   167→| Item | Section | Effort | Why first |
   168→|------|---------|--------|-----------|
   169→| PyPI packaging | Packaging | Medium | Gateway to all adoption |
   170→| Zero-config launch | Packaging | Low | First-run magic moment |
   171→| Local-first privacy | Packaging | Low | Trust signal, 1-day effort |
   172→| Cost estimation | Cost Tracking | Medium | #1 requested feature |
   173→| Cowork parser | Agent Parsers | Low | Doubles addressable audience |
   174→| Shareable session links | Sharing | Medium | Viral growth loop |
   175→
   176→### Tier 2: Do Next (HIGH pop. + Moderate, or MED pop. + Straightforward)
   177→
   178→| Item | Section | Effort | Why next |
   179→|------|---------|--------|---------|
   180→| Frontend asset bundling | Packaging | Medium | Required for PyPI |
   181→| Session highlights | Session Insights | Medium | Spotify Wrapped for agents |
   182→| Friction detection | Session Insights | Medium | Universally engaging |
   183→| Burn rate projection | Cost Tracking | Medium | Budget planning |
   184→| Incremental parsing | Performance | Medium | Eliminates startup pain |
   185→| Conversation flow diagrams | Session Graphs | Medium | Screenshot-worthy |
   186→| MCP server integration | Agent Parsers | Medium | Hot ecosystem standard |
   187→| Session continuation chains | Session Graphs | Low | Quick win |
   188→| Virtual scrolling | Performance | Low | Quick win |
   189→| Data retention policies | Privacy | Low | Quick win |
   190→| Guided onboarding | Skill Coaching | Medium | Reduces new-user churn |
   191→
   192→### Tier 3: Backlog (MED pop. + Moderate, or LOW pop.)
   193→
   194→Everything else — valuable but not urgent.
   195→
   196→---
   197→
   198→## Items Removed from Roadmap
   199→
   200→The following items were removed based on feasibility assessment and population analysis (would the feature attract many users?):
   201→
   202→| Removed Item | Reason |
   203→|-------------|--------|
   204→| 5-hour rolling window | Niche rate-limit concern; most users care about total cost, not rate-limit blocks |
   205→| Cache efficiency audit | Micro-optimization; only relevant to users actively tuning prompt caching |
   206→| Compaction analytics | Claude Code-specific implementation detail; narrow audience |
   207→| Context window utilization | Too technical; most users don't think in terms of context budgets |
   208→| Methodology fingerprinting | Academic; classifying problem-solving strategies has unclear practical value |
   209→| Session quality scoring | Replaced by outcome scoring (more intuitive, inspired by `/insights`) |
   210→| Cross-session diff | Rarely needed; challenging to define meaningful diff semantics across sessions |
   211→| 24-week activity board | Minor variant of existing heatmap; low incremental value |
   212→| Skill extraction | Vague — "auto-identify reusable techniques" lacks clear implementation path |
   213→| Session-based coaching | Depends on too many unbuilt prerequisites; premature |
   214→| Jira / Linear sync | Enterprise complexity (bidirectional sync, webhooks, per-org config) for few users |
   215→| Swarm topology visualization | Complex D3 graph rendering with unclear user value for current audience size |
   216→| Task routing recommendations | Requires ML training data and inference pipeline; premature |
   217→| Consensus & conflict detection | Needs semantic comparison infrastructure; research-grade complexity |
   218→| Knowledge transfer friction | Vague metric with no clear measurement methodology |
   219→| Session clustering | Requires embedding model integration and heavy ML dependencies |
   220→| Contextual redaction | Needs NLP pipeline for semantic understanding; overengineered vs. regex-based auditing |
   221→| Anomaly detection (security) | Requires statistical baseline infrastructure; premature for current scale |
   222→| VS Code extension | Entirely separate project with its own release cycle and maintenance burden |
   223→| Personal knowledge base | Scope creep — separate product territory beyond observability |
   224→| Memory-mapped JSONL reading | Marginal performance gain with OS-specific complexity; not worth the investment |
   225→| Interactive tool call DAG | Replaced by session relationship DAG which provides more actionable insights |
   226→| ML & predictive features | All prediction-based items removed per project direction — focus on descriptive analytics |
   227→| Aider / Cursor / Windsurf / Kimi parsers | Out of scope for this stage; revisit after core parser ecosystem stabilizes |

   236→- [Cowork](https://www.anthropic.com/) — Anthropic's desktop agent (GUI wrapper over Claude Code)
